the differentiator. That's the thing really that's going to make me all the money. Then you're going to work more on the bill product and the bill presentation than you probably have up until now. I gave you a bunch of reference stuff. I'm only going to mention a few things about it. This is your page 11. It's right out of the Ogilvy book. The underlying line is in the early years of the agency, Ogilvy affected a full-length flowing black cape with a scarlet lining. This is how he went to meetings. This is how he came to the office. I think the key words are in the early years because some people might maybe develop the arrogance to do that upon achieving a certain level of status. He understood that you get the status by doing it. It happens in the opposite direction. I want you to think about this, by the way. This is 1950s. This is young punk, upstart, new guy on Madison Avenue going to meetings in a bat cape. You didn't even see this on Mad Men, by the way. They left it out because it's too weird. When I stopped to think about it, Halbert comes out at the seminar. He pays $7,000 a piece to be in a room. Keep in mind this is 25 years ago, so you guys got a great bargain. In today's prices, that'd be $35,000 adjusted for inflation. Now we're in Key West, so Halbert comes out in shorts and sneakers and one of them mesh-like t-shirt things. Baseball cap says, clients suck, and he hadn't shaved. It's the cape. It's the cape. More do it than don't do it. I shot a TV show, Infomercial, with Robert Wagner. Bob now, as he describes it, he has been for many years in the business of being Robert Wagner, not really acting. He makes his living as Robert Wagner. Bob's pretty open about it, in that the fee's $100,000 and it really doesn't matter what. Sometimes he gets paid $100,000 to go play golf with three guys because they want to spend the day playing golf with Bob Wagner. Sometimes he gets it to do an infomercial. It doesn't matter to him. One's the same as the other. We were talking about, so the first day he shows up for rehearsals. I'm watching the arrival and it is clearly choreographed and practiced and the jacket over the shoulder just so. I still had the effect, by the way, but analytically, it's like clear to me he has done this before and done it exactly in the way that he is doing it now. We were talking about it later. He said, oh, he said, that's my movie star entrance. People would be disappointed if they didn't get the movie star entrance. We were talking about wardrobe. He said, I'll bring my TV uniform. I said, it's a blue blazer. I'll bring a double breasted one, a single breasted one, you pick blue shirt. I bring four ties to choose from and I'll have a pair of gray slacks. That's my TV uniform. Is that okay? He's got a uniform. All of this is very thoughtful. It's very on purpose, calculated at one point and then just ingrained. I doubt he really has to think about the entrance anymore, but he does it. Ogilvy, the other page I gave you, a sure instinct for the memorable gesture.
